ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for ground tarragon spice; SNOMED CT coverage for culinary herbs is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of ground tarragon is approximately 1 teaspoon (about 1.5 g), used as a seasoning in sauces, dressings, and meat dishes.

nutrient_source.title: Spices, tarragon, dried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 02038)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for dried tarragon (Artemisia dracunculus) is the exact match for ground tarragon from dried leaves. HPB Singapore source values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, and iron are consistent with USDA data and are retained as primary values. USDA data used to supplement saturated fat, sugar, and additional micronutrients not reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: Estimated GI for dried ground tarragon based on analogous dried herb/spice category; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for ground tarragon in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at approximately 15 (low) based on the analogous dried herb/spice category. Dried herbs and spices are predominantly consumed in very small quantities (1–2 g per serving); the glycemic load per serving is negligible at 0.11. This estimate is consistent with the low-GI classification applied to all dried culinary herbs in peer-reviewed GI literature. GL per serving calculated as: GI (15) × carbohydrate per serving (50.22 g/100g × 1.5 g serving = 0.75 g) / 100 = 0.11.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (62 mg/100g) with exceptionally high potassium (3020 mg/100g); potassium-to-sodium ratio is approximately 49:1, strongly favourable for blood pressure management.
hypertension.guidance: Ground tarragon is suitable for individuals with hypertension; used in typical culinary quantities of 1–2 g per serving, the absolute sodium contribution is negligible and the high potassium content supports vasodilation and blood pressure reduction — avoid tarragon-based spice blends that contain added salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (estimated ~15), high dietary fibre (7.4 g/100g), and bioactive polyphenols (rutin, quercetin, kaempferol) with evidence of insulin-sensitising properties; carbohydrate contribution per culinary serving is negligible.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ground tarragon is suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes; the estimated GI is low and a typical 1.5 g serving contributes less than 0.8 g of carbohydrate, making glycaemic impact negligible — avoid tarragon-flavoured commercial products that may contain added sugar or high-GI fillers.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.88 g/100g), zero cholesterol, high potassium (3020 mg/100g), and rich polyphenol content (quercetin, kaempferol) with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties relevant to cardiovascular risk reduction.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ground tarragon is suitable for individuals with cardiovascular disease; its negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and high potassium content support heart health, and the polyphenol profile may contribute to reduced oxidative stress — use as a flavour enhancer to reduce reliance on salt in cooking.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Rich in anti-inflammatory polyphenols (rutin, quercetin, kaempferol) and vitamin C (50 mg/100g); exceptionally high calcium (1139 mg/100g) and potassium (3020 mg/100g) support bone health critical for RA patients on corticosteroids.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ground tarragon is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; its polyphenol and vitamin C content may support reduction of inflammatory markers, and its high calcium content is beneficial for bone density maintenance in patients on long-term corticosteroid therapy — avoid pre-mixed blends with added salt or anti-caking agents.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ground tarragon is a dried and finely milled culinary herb derived from the leaves of Artemisia dracunculus, commonly known as French tarragon. It presents as a fine green-grey powder with a distinctive sweet, anise-like aroma and flavour profile attributed to the volatile compound estragole (methyl chavicol). Ground tarragon is a cornerstone herb in French cuisine, integral to the classic fines herbes blend alongside chervil, chives, and parsley, and is widely used in béarnaise sauce, chicken dishes, vinaigrettes, and cream-based sauces. Nutritionally, ground tarragon is exceptionally dense per 100 g: it provides 295 kcal, 22.77 g protein, 7.24 g fat (of which 1.88 g is saturated), and 50.22 g carbohydrate with 7.4 g dietary fibre. It is extraordinarily rich in potassium (3020 mg/100g), calcium (1139 mg/100g), and iron (32.3 mg/100g), and contains meaningful levels of vitamin C (50 mg/100g), folate (274 mcg DFE/100g), and B vitamins. However, because ground tarragon is used in very small culinary quantities — typically 1–2 g per serving — the per-100g figures are not representative of actual nutrient intake per meal. Its glycemic index is estimated at approximately 15 (low), and a typical serving contributes negligible carbohydrate and glycaemic load. Clinically, ground tarragon is suitable for individuals managing hypertension (very low sodium, outstanding potassium-to-sodium ratio), type 2 diabetes (low GI, insulin-sensitising polyphenols), cardiovascular disease (low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, antioxidant polyphenols), and rheumatoid arthritis (anti-inflammatory flavonoids, high calcium for bone health). It is vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher.
common_user_queries: What is ground tarragon used for?
What does ground tarragon taste like?
How much tarragon should I use per serving?
Is ground tarragon the same as dried tarragon?
What herbs are in fines herbes?
What is the GI of tarragon?

ingredient_synonyms.fines herbes: classic French herb blend including tarragon

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore (primary regional source) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170934) for dried tarragon. Both sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy. Values are consistent across both sources. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) as no direct GI measurement exists for ground tarragon.
notes: 1. Sugar_g is reported as 0.0 g per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for dried tarragon; this is a confirmed near-zero value consistent with the drying and grinding process. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA). Vitamin_b12_mcg and vitamin_d_mcg are 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based food with no animal-derived components). 2. Monounsaturated_fat_g (0.47 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (3.69 g), and all micronutrient values supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy where not reported by HPB. 3. GI of approximately 15 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for ground tarragon exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. The estimate is based on the dried herb/spice category in Atkinson et al. (2008). 4. The dietary_tag 'wholegrain' from the input record has been removed as it is not applicable to a dried herb/spice. 5. Occasion_tag 'snack' from the input record has been removed as it is not representative of typical tarragon use. 6. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03301710 is the closest defensible match for tarragon herb; FoodEx2 code A00LN is used for dried/ground tarragon. These codes should be verified against the latest FoodOn and FoodEx2 release versions. 7. No SNOMED CT code identified for ground tarragon as a culinary spice.
